subcategory: West African culinary medicinal bitter shrub

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in zone 9–12; Bitter Leaf is a large, fast-growing African shrub (formerly Vernonia amygdalina) — one of the most important culinary and medicinal plants in West and Central Africa — with large, soft, dark-green leaves that are processed by washing and kneading to reduce bitterness before use in classic West African soups including Ofe Onugbu and Bitter Leaf Soup, and used widely as a medicinal herb across the continent.

directions: Root Gymnanthemum amygdalinum (Bitter Leaf) stem cuttings in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; plants establish quickly and grow vigorously in warm climates — prune regularly to maintain a compact, shrubby form and encourage dense, tender leaf production; older stems become woody and less productive. Bitter Leaf (formerly Vernonia amygdalina) is arguably the most important vegetable shrub in West and Central African cuisine — a fast-growing, multi-purpose plant grown in every kitchen garden from Nigeria to the DRC, valued equally as a culinary ingredient, medicinal herb, and forage plant. The leaves contain bitter sesquiterpene lactones (vernodalin, vernomygdin) that give the plant its distinctive medicinal and flavor properties; for culinary use, fresh leaves are washed thoroughly, squeezed and kneaded repeatedly in water, and the liquid pressed out to reduce bitterness to a palatable level before adding to soups, stews, and sauces. The squeezed leaves add a distinctive savory, slightly bitter depth to Ofe Onugbu (Igbo bitter leaf soup with cocoyam and assorted meats) and other traditional dishes. In tropical Africa, the plant is also used to treat malaria, diabetes, fever, and intestinal parasites; research has identified antihelminthic and hypoglycemic compounds. In zone 9–12, Bitter Leaf grows as a perennial shrub reaching 4–8 feet; in cooler areas it can be grown as an annual in a large container.
sources: West African Cookbooks — Ofe Onugbu (Bitter Leaf Soup) reference

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in deep to partial shade in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in zone 4–9; Japanese Fairy Bells is a graceful woodland perennial from Japan, China, and Korea — producing arching, leafy stems and small, nodding, tubular cream-white flowers in spring followed by glossy black berries in fall — a refined addition to shade gardens alongside hostas, ferns, and Solomon's seals.

directions: Plant Disporum sessile in humus-rich, evenly moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in part to deep shade; mulch with compost or leaf mold to maintain cool, moist soil; divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants spread slowly by rhizomes to form attractive colonies. Japanese Fairy Bells is one of several Asian woodland plants that bring charm to the deeply shaded garden — related to the North American Disporum (Fairy Bells) but native to the rich forest floors of Japan, China, and Korea. The arching stems bear paired, ovate, ribbed leaves and small clusters of nodding, narrow, tubular, cream-white flowers at the tips in spring, followed in late summer and fall by small, round, glossy black berries. Several cultivars have been selected for exceptional foliage: 'Ki-fukurin' has gold-edged leaves, and various variegated forms are prized by collectors of Japanese woodland plants. The spreading rhizome system slowly colonizes woodland settings and pairs beautifully with hostas, hellebores, ferns, and other denizens of the shaded garden. Hardy and reliable in zone 4–9, going dormant in winter and re-emerging from the spreading rhizome system in spring.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil in zone 9–11; Wallichia disticha (Distichous Fishtail Palm) is a striking, unusual Himalayan palm with a unique flat, fan-like crown — all fronds held in a single plane, giving a two-dimensional silhouette — native to shaded forest margins from Nepal and northeastern India to southern China, and notable for being monocarpic (each stem flowers once, then dies, while the clump persists).

directions: Plant Wallichia disticha in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in bright partial shade, protecting from harsh afternoon sun and cold below 28°F; water regularly during establishment and provide a balanced slow-release palm fertilizer in spring and summer. The Distichous Fishtail Palm is one of the most architecturally distinctive of all palms — its unique flat crown, with all fronds arranged in a single vertical plane like pages in an open book (the meaning of 'distichous'), creates a dramatic, two-dimensional silhouette unlike any other palm species. Native to the moist, seasonally cool forests of the eastern Himalayas from Nepal through northeast India to southern China and Myanmar, it is adapted to partial shade and cooler conditions than most palms — making it useful in gardens where more tender palms fail. Wallichia disticha is monocarpic like most fishtail palms: each individual stem flowers and fruits once then dies, but the clump produces multiple stems and the overall plant persists. It is not as commonly cultivated as many other palms but is prized by collectors for its unusual form; it makes a spectacular specimen in zone 9–11 gardens in a sheltered, partially shaded position with reliable moisture.

subcategory: tidal mangrove edible palm Southeast Asia

planting.note: Plant propagules or container stock in spring in full sun in tidal or permanently wet, brackish to fresh, low-salinity soil or at the edge of still, warm water in zone 10–12; Nipa Palm is a remarkable, stemless, tidal palm native to mangrove estuaries from the Indian subcontinent through Southeast Asia and the Pacific — one of the oldest flowering plants on Earth with a fossil record of 70 million years — grown for its edible young endosperm (kaong), palm wine tapped from the flowering stalk, and leaves used for traditional thatching (nipa huts) throughout Southeast Asia.

directions: Plant Nypa fruticans propagules (the large, floating seeds germinate readily and can be pressed into waterlogged soil) in tidal freshwater to slightly brackish mudflats or pond margins with permanently saturated soil in full sun; the rhizome spreads horizontally underground and produces new upright frond clusters periodically; plants tolerate some saltwater intrusion but grow best in low-salinity or freshwater tidal conditions. Nipa Palm is one of the most ancient and ecologically important palms in the world — with a fossil record extending back 70 million years to the Cretaceous period — and is the only member of the subfamily Nypoideae, making it botanically unique among all living palms. Unlike all other palms, Nypa is essentially stemless: its massive, horizontal trunk lies underground or just at the water surface, sending up large, pinnate fronds 10–15 feet tall directly from the horizontal stem. The spherical, rugose fruit clusters yield young, jelly-like endosperm (kaong or palm toddy in the Philippines) eaten fresh or preserved in syrup; the developing floral stalks are tapped for sap (up to 8 liters/day) fermented into palm wine (tuba) or boiled to make palm sugar (gula atap). Millions of acres of Nipa stands form critical tidal mangrove habitat across Southeast Asia, storing substantial carbon and protecting coastlines from erosion. Cultivation outside its natural range requires a very wet, frost-free environment.
sources: FAO — Nypa fruticans production and uses

planting.note: Plant container stock in deep to partial shade in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in zone 10–12; the Joey Palm (Johannesteijsmannia magnifica) is one of the most spectacular of all shade palms — endemic to the rainforest floors of Peninsular Malaysia and Borneo — producing among the largest undivided leaves of any palm, each enormous diamond-shaped frond reaching 10–15 feet long and 6–8 feet wide, in deep, humid forest shade.

directions: Grow Johannesteijsmannia magnifica in deep to bright indirect shade in moist, humus-rich, acidic, very well-drained soil with high organic matter; maintain consistently high humidity (80%+), protect from any direct sun (the large leaves scorch badly in direct light), and never allow the root zone to dry; in zone 10–12, this is a slow-growing but supremely architectural shade garden specimen. The Joey Palm is arguably the most spectacular of all ornamental understory palms for tropical gardens — producing magnificent, enormous, diamond- or rhombus-shaped, undivided fronds each ribbed with prominent veins on a stout petiole, creating a bold tropical statement in the shaded garden. It grows strictly as a forest floor specialist in its native Peninsular Malaysia and Borneo, never breaking into direct sun, and requires analogous conditions in cultivation: dense shade, high humidity, consistently moist soil, and warm temperatures above 50°F at all times. There are only four species in the Johannesteijsmannia genus — all endemic to Sundaland and all similar in their enormous, undivided fronds — with J. magnifica being the largest and most dramatic. All are threatened or endangered in the wild due to forest clearance. In zone 10–12, plant under the canopy of large trees or on the north or east side of a building where shade is assured; it makes an unrivaled specimen in tropical rain garden settings.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, coarse or sandy-loam soil in zone 9–11; Lepidozamia peroffskyana (Pineapple Zamia) is a magnificent, large cycad native to subtropical and tropical Queensland rainforest margins in Australia — producing very long, glossy, arching, pinnate fronds in a large, impressive crown and enormous female cones resembling pineapples that are among the largest of any cycad.

directions: Plant Lepidozamia peroffskyana in well-drained, coarse soil (sandy loam with added grit or perlite) in partial shade to full sun; avoid overwatering and ensure excellent drainage as cycads are highly susceptible to root rot in waterlogged soil; apply a slow-release cycad fertilizer with minor elements in spring. Caution: all parts of Lepidozamia peroffskyana are toxic — seeds contain cycasin and cause severe neurological damage if ingested — keep away from children and livestock. Pineapple Zamia is one of the most impressive cycads in cultivation — native to subtropical and warm-temperate rainforest margins in coastal Queensland — and produces a stately, fountain-like crown of very long, glossy-green, arching pinnate fronds that can reach 8–10 feet in length. The name 'Pineapple Zamia' refers to the massive female reproductive cones, which can weigh 20–35 kg (44–77 lbs) and closely resemble giant pineapples. Like all cycads, it is a living fossil — cycads have changed little since the Mesozoic Era when they formed a significant part of dinosaur-era forests. Growth is slow: adding one new flush of fronds per year; a mature, cone-bearing specimen in a garden represents decades of patient cultivation. The striking glossy fronds and bold architectural form make it a supreme specimen plant for subtropical gardens throughout zone 9–11.
sources: PACSOA (Palm and Cycad Societies of Australia)
